coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic removal of NO by Z-scheme mineral based heterojunction intermediated by carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiazhang
Shi, Haiyang
Wang, Tianshi
Zhang, Yuying
Zuo, Shixiang
Luo, Shiping
Yao, Chao
coredata.dc:description: A novel photocatalyst integrated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs), SmFeO3 and attapulgite (ATP) clay mineral was synthesized by sol-gel/impregnation method which was successfully applied for NO removal under solar light irradiation. The textual and optical properties of the materials were analyzed by various characterizations. Results indicated the SmFeO3 nanoparticles were uniformed loaded on the ATP support and both coherently interacted with CQDs. The amount of CQDs had significant effect on the NO conversion, while 5 wt% incorporation achieved the highest 90% conversion rate. An indirect Z-scheme mechanism was proposed that the CQDs performed as an intermediate between SmFeO3 and ATP for the charge transfer while the high redox potential was maintained. In addition, the ATP fiber showed not only UV-responsive property in Z-scheme structure, but also excellent adsorption effect for gas molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Attapulgite
Perovskite
NO conversion
Z-scheme photocatalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China ( 51674043 , 51702026 ), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology ( BM2012110 ), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), Jiangsu Technology Support Program ( BE2015103 , BE2016654 ) and Social Development Fund of Changzhou City ( CE20165022 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TG-DSC curves of as-prepared SmFeO3/ATP gel.
XRD patterns of ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP, CQDs and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP.
TEM and HRTEM images of (a, b) CQDs, (c, d) SmFeO3/ATP, (e, f) 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP.
FT-IR spectra of ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP, CQDs and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP.
XPS spectra of ATP, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposite (a) survey of the samples, (b) Sm 3d, (c) Fe 2p, (d) O 1 s, (e) Si 2p and (f) C 1 s.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of all samples and (b) band gap energy of ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP.
(a) PL spectra and (b) transient photocurrent response of SmFeO3, ATP, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP.
Photocatalytic oxidation of NO conversion efficiency (a) and selectivity (b) with irradiation time.
VB-XPS spectra of ATP and SmFeO3.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanisms of the as-prepared CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposites: (a) under visible light irradiation and (b) under simulated solar light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic removal of NO by Z-scheme mineral based heterojunction intermediated by carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Delaware, Newark, DE 19716, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China.(X. Li)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel photocatalyst integrated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs), SmFeO3 and attapulgite (ATP) clay mineral was synthesized by sol-gel/impregnation method which was successfully applied for NO removal under solar light irradiation. The textual and optical properties of the materials were analyzed by various characterizations. Results indicated the SmFeO3 nanoparticles were uniformed loaded on the ATP support and both coherently interacted with CQDs. The amount of CQDs had significant effect on the NO conversion, while 5 wt% incorporation achieved the highest 90% conversion rate. An indirect Z-scheme mechanism was proposed that the CQDs performed as an intermediate between SmFeO3 and ATP for the charge transfer while the high redox potential was maintained. In addition, the ATP fiber showed not only UV-responsive property in Z-scheme structure, but also excellent adsorption effect for gas molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the primary air pollutant, NO imposes health risk for humans including respiratory diseases, acid rain, photochemical smog and solid particles PM2.5. Therefore, efficient methods for control of NO emission are urgently needed. It is widely accepted that photocatalysis has become a burgeoning approach for the pollution abatement [1–3], which is expected to be an environment-friendly, cost-effective and efficient oxidation process for NOx removal. Up to date, strenuous efforts have been put to employ semiconductor nanomaterials for photocatalytic degradation of NOx [4,5]. For instance, M.J. Hernández Rodríguez et al. [6] synthesized a TiO2-based photocatalyst coupled with gold by sol-gel method, and found that 0.3 wt% Au modified TiO2 oxidized NO to NO2 with high degradation rates. Huang et al. [7] obtained hierarchical porous ZnWO4 microspheres by ultrasonic-spray pyrolysis for NOx removal. Wang et al. [8] has prepared layer-structured Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction for photocatalytic of NO in air. In particular, perovskite-type oxides have exhibited great potential due to its high catalytic activity, strong oxidizing ability and chemical stability [9,10]. Although photocatalytic activity of perovskite-type oxides have been extensively studied, such as pure LaMnO3 [11], Cu doped LaCoO3 [12], Ag/LaFeO3 [13] etc, few studies have been reported on NOx photocatalytic conversion. In our previous work [14,15], attapulgite clay supported LaFeO3 perovskite oxides doped with Mn was obtained, and found that the formation of “solid solution/coprecipitation” heterostructure demonstrated excellent photo-selective catalytic reduction of NOx. As another important member in perovskite family, SmFeO3 owns narrow bandgap which displays promising photocatalytic activity for degradation of inorganic and organic pollutants [16]. However, the pure SmFeO3 has considerable drawbacks such as large particle size, fast recombination of electron-hole pairs and poor adsorptive performance [17,18]. As a natural clay mineral, ATP has been known with abundant active sites and large specific surface area, making it as a good candidate for absorbent and the supported catalyst [19]. More importantly, pure ATP has been reported to exhibit absorption in the ultraviolet light region which enable it as a UV responsive semiconductor to some extent [20]. Thus, it is feasible to couple SmFeO3 with ATP to form SmFeO3/ATP heterojunction, which favors the efficient separation of electrons and holes.

On the other hand, generally more negative conduction band (CB) potential and more positive valence band (VB) potential render higher reduction and oxidation capabilities [21,22]. For the traditional type of semiconductor heterojunctions, the obvious disadvantage is that the oxidation-reduction capabilities of photogenerated electrons and holes are weakened after charge transfer. Given the virtue of efficient spatial separation of electrons and holes pairs, artificial heterogeneous Z-scheme systems maintained with strong reduction/oxidation abilities can be realized [23]. Among all Z-scheme photocatalytic systems, all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst by using solid electron mediators including noble-metal (Pt, Au, Ag) particles as electron mediators have been explored extensively [24], however the high cost and complex preparation limits their wide applications. Alternatively, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much more attention due to its low cost and easy accessibility [25,26]. In recent years, CQDs have been proven to be an excellent solid electron mediator in the Z-scheme photocatalytic systems, such as BiVO4/CQDs/CdS and CQDs/WO3, demonstrating enhanced photocatalytic activities for water splitting and photocatalytic hydrogen production, respectively [27,28]. Therefore the CQDs may play significant role in the mineral based Z-scheme nanocomposite materials by efficiently achieving high redox potential thus enhance the photocatalytic activity.

In the present work, we rationally designed all-solid-state CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP Z-scheme nanocomposites which possessed high oxidation-reduction potential on photocatalytic oxidation NO. Moreover, the effect of CQDs on photocatalytic oxidation rate of NO under solar light irradiation is comprehensively investigated by tuning the loading amounts of CQDs. Finally, the possible electron transfer and NO oxidation reactions in CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP photocatalyst are investigated to unravel the reaction mechanism.

Sm(NO3)3·6H2O and Fe(NO3)3·6H2O were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. ATP was provided from Jiangsu Nanda Zijin Technology Group Co., Ltd. (Changzhou, China). C6H8O7·H2O and (CH2OH)2 were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized according to the method reported in the literature [29]. Typically, 0.5254 g (2.5 mmol) citric acid was dissolved in 12 mL deionized water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160 °C in an electric oven and kept for 8 h. The upper solution was collected and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The solid CQDs can be obtained by freeze-drying.

SmFeO3/ATP was synthesized by a sol-gel method. 1.75 g of samarium nitrate, 1.59 g of iron nitrate and 1.6 g of citric acid were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water and put into 250 mL breaker, then 100 mL of deionized water and 3 mL ethylene glycol were added to the mixture under ultrasonic dissolving. The mixed solution was maintained at 80 °C under stirring for one hour, followed by the addition of 1 g of ATP. The composite catalyst was obtained after calcined at 600 °C for 2 h.

CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP were synthesized by an impregnation method. In a typical preparation, 1 g of SmFeO3/ATP was added into 10 mL water after ultrasonic stirring for 1 h at room temperature. 0.01 g of CQDs is added to the as-obtained SmFeO3/ATP solution by magnetically stirring for 30 min and impregnated for 24 h. The resulting solid was collected by filtration, and dried at 60 °C. The obtained powder was denoted as 1 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposite. Hereafter, the other samples with different mass of CQDs (2 wt%, 3 wt%, 4 wt%, 5 wt%, 6 wt% and 7 wt%) were prepared using the same method.

The XRD patterns were taken on a X-ray diffractometer equipped with a Cu anode (D/Max 2500 PC, Rgaku Corporation, Japan), running at 60 kV and 30 mA with 2θ range between 10° and 80° at a scan rate of 0.05° per second. The TEM images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (JEM–2100, JEOL, Japan), working at 200 kV. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra were obtained for the dry-pressed disk samples using a Scanning UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS, UV-2700, Japan) equipped with an integrating sphere assembly, using 100% BaSO4 as a reflectance sample. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was carried on a fluorescence photometer (LS45) with the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were used on a Spectrum 100 spectrometer (PE, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy with Al-KαX-ray (hv = 1486.6 eV) radiation operated at 150 W (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250, USA) was used to investigate the surface properties. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded at ambient temperature by a JES FA200 spectrometer. The photoelectric analysis was performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C, CHI Shanghai, Inc.) using a standard three-electrode system. The 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was chosen as the electrolyte and a 300 W xenon lamp as the light source.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated by the removal of NO in a continuous flow reactor (Fig. S1). The experiment was conducted with PFD-5000 light-heat fixed bed reactor provided by Huasi Instrument Co., Ltd. (Hunan, China) and KM9106 flue gas analyzer purchased by Kahn International Co., Ltd. The light source was a 500 W xenon lamp which can simulate sunlight. The inlet gas composition was as follows: 1000 ppb NO and 5 vol% O2 with N2 as balance gas. The operational conditions of the photoactivity tests were as follows: space velocity (SV) of 3000 h−1, the total gas flow rate of 15 mL/min and 2 h of illumination. Two open windows on both sides of the reactor chamber were conducive to the light source irradiation. The concentration of gas was by flue NOx detector recorded once every 10 min during the reaction. The percentages of removed [NO] (NO/%) and selectivity of NO oxidation to ionic species (S) in the reaction are calculated as follows [6]: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.06.133.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated by the removal of NO in a continuous flow reactor (Fig. S1 ). The experiment was conducted with PFD-5000 light-heat fixed bed reactor provided by Huasi Instrument Co., Ltd. (Hunan, China) and KM9106 flue gas analyzer purchased by Kahn International Co., Ltd. The light source was a 500 W xenon lamp which can simulate sunlight. The inlet gas composition was as follows: 1000 ppb NO and 5 vol% O2 with N2 as balance gas. The operational conditions of the photoactivity tests were as follows: space velocity (SV) of 3000 h−1, the total gas flow rate of 15 mL/min and 2 h of illumination. Two open windows on both sides of the reactor chamber were conducive to the light source irradiation. The concentration of gas was by flue NOx detector recorded once every 10 min during the reaction. The percentages of removed [NO] (NO/%) and selectivity of NO oxidation to ionic species (S) in the reaction are calculated as follows [6]:

(2)
S = 1 -
NO 2
out
NO
in
-
NO
out
Where [NO]in is the NO concentration after 10 min of adsorption or equilibrium in the dark and [NO]out and [NO2]out are the concentrations at the end of the illumination period. All concentrations are expressed in molarities.

In order to get insight of the formation mechanism of the SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposite, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is conducted and the thermograms is shown in Fig. 1 . The peak between 50 and 140 °C at a maximum of 100 °C with a weight loss around 5% is observed, mainly due to the evaporation of physisorbed water. It can be found that the next mass loss is from 140 °C to 350 °C at a maximum of 260 °C with the weight loss of around 45%, which may be attributed to the loss of oligomers and the subsequent rapid mass loss occurred due to the pyrolysis of citric acid linked material [30]. Furthermore, the curve between 350 and 550 °C at a maximum of 390 °C associated with a weight loss about 20% is observed, attributed to the decomposition of residue organics and intermediates. The curve between 550 and 800 °C with a trace weight loss of 3% corresponds to further removal of the remaining crystalline water by dihydroxylation, where no obvious weight loss occurs with increasing the thermal treatment temperature to 800 °C. It indicates that highly thermal stable materials can be formed at 600 °C, which is consistent with our previous result [31].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP, CQDs and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP are shown in Fig. 2 . The crystal structure and symmetry of each sample are deduced by referring to the standard powder diffraction file (PDF) in the database of the International Centre of Diffraction Data (ICDD). The diffraction peak of ATP at 26.6° is ascribed to the quartz SiO2 [19]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of SmFeO3 occur at 22.87°, 25.63° and 32.71°, which can be attributed to the (1 0 1), (1 1 1), (1 2 1) crystal planes of rhombohedra SmFeO3, respectively. It can be seen that the sample of SmFeO3 agrees well with the standard card (JCPDS Card No.39-1490). As for SmFeO3/ATP, the intensity of the characteristic peaks near 26.6° corresponding to ATP is weakened and no other impurities are identified, indicating that SmFeO3 has been loaded onto the surface of ATP. The distinctive peak of pure CQDs at 23° can be indexed to the (1 0 1) planes of the graphite structures. However, no characteristic peak of carbon can be identified in the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP composite due to the low crystallinity of CQDs, indicating that the CQDs are highly dispersed on the surface of SmFeO3/ATP [32].

The morphology of CQDs, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP were investigated by TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 3 (a, b) show the typical TEM and HRTEM image of pure CQDs particles which are uniformly dispersed with an average size of about 5 nm. The observed spacing distance between the lattice planes is measured to be 0.218 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 1) crystallographic planes of graphite [29]. Fig. 3(c) shows the TEM picture of SmFeO3/ATP that the SmFeO3 particles with an average size ca. 13 nm are uniformly loaded on the surface of ATP rods without obvious aggregation. Such a small size of perovskite particles may be formed due to the presence of abundant active sites on the ATP surface favoring the controllable growth of perovskite grains, and similar phenomena have been found in our previous work [14]. Fig. 3(d) shows the lattice fringe of d = 0.274 nm on the ATP rods which matches well with the crystallographic plane (1 2 1) of SmFeO3. Fig. 3(e, f) show the TEM images of the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP composite, where CQDs and SmFeO3 are deposited on the surface of the ATP rods substrate, indicating that the composite has two distinctive morphologies of nanoparticles. From the inset HRTEM image taken in the randomly selected area, it can be seen that CQDs and SmFeO3 with different fringes could be observed and the CQDs are tightly jointed between SmFeO3 and ATP, which benefit for the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction. Fig. S2 provides EDS and dark-field TEM image of 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP, where the Si, Mg, Al elements can be attributed to the ATP in Fig. S2(a), and the perovskites were loaded on the ATP surface as seen in Fig. S2(b).

The morphology of CQDs, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP were investigated by TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 3 (a, b) show the typical TEM and HRTEM image of pure CQDs particles which are uniformly dispersed with an average size of about 5 nm. The observed spacing distance between the lattice planes is measured to be 0.218 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 1) crystallographic planes of graphite [29]. Fig. 3(c) shows the TEM picture of SmFeO3/ATP that the SmFeO3 particles with an average size ca. 13 nm are uniformly loaded on the surface of ATP rods without obvious aggregation. Such a small size of perovskite particles may be formed due to the presence of abundant active sites on the ATP surface favoring the controllable growth of perovskite grains, and similar phenomena have been found in our previous work [14]. Fig. 3(d) shows the lattice fringe of d = 0.274 nm on the ATP rods which matches well with the crystallographic plane (1 2 1) of SmFeO3. Fig. 3(e, f) show the TEM images of the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP composite, where CQDs and SmFeO3 are deposited on the surface of the ATP rods substrate, indicating that the composite has two distinctive morphologies of nanoparticles. From the inset HRTEM image taken in the randomly selected area, it can be seen that CQDs and SmFeO3 with different fringes could be observed and the CQDs are tightly jointed between SmFeO3 and ATP, which benefit for the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction. Fig. S2 provides EDS and dark-field TEM image of 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP, where the Si, Mg, Al elements can be attributed to the ATP in Fig. S2(a), and the perovskites were loaded on the ATP surface as seen in Fig. S2(b).

The FT-IR absorption spectra of ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP, CQDs and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP are shown in Fig. 4 . As for pure ATP, the characteristic peak at 3561 cm−1 is attributed to the stretch vibration of structural OH, the absorption bands in 1436 and 1645 cm−1 are caused by the bend vibration of adsorbed water, and the peaks at 476 and 1002 cm−1 are attributed to bend vibrations of Si (or Al)−O tetrahedral [33]. In pure SmFeO3, a strong peak at 560 cm−1 corresponds to FeO vibrations of FeO6 octahedral in SmFeO3 structure [17]. It can be found the characteristic peaks of both ATP and SmFeO3 can be identified in SmFeO3/ATP sample, indicating that SmFeO3 is successfully loaded on ATP. As far as CQDs are concerned, it contains several functional groups including OH (3431 cm−1), CH (2893 cm−1), CO (1698 cm−1), CC (1438 cm−1) and CO (1159 cm−1) [32], whereas the absorption band at 1698 and 1438 cm−1 in 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP become weaker than pure CQDs, suggesting the existence of C-bonding with SmFeO3/ATP surface. However, there appears stretching vibrations of Si (or Al)-O group in 1008 cm−1 and OH group in 1633 cm−1 which are stronger in 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP than SmFeO3/ATP. On the contrary, they are weaker than others in ATP, indicating that the CQDs have interface interaction between ATP and SmFeO3, which is consistent with the TEM analysis.

Fig. 5 shows the XPS spectra of ATP, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposite, which are calibrated by using the impurity C 1 s (284.8 eV). Compared with the survey scan spectrum of ATP and SmFeO3/ATP in Fig. 5(a), the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP contains Sm, Fe, Mg, Al, Si, O and C, which is consistent with the results of EDS. As for the SmFeO3/ATP sample displayed in Fig. 5(b), the Sm 3d spectra consist of two peaks with binding energies around 1083 and 1110 eV, corresponding to the signals from the doublets of Sm 3d5/2 and Sm 3d3/2 in the trivalent oxidation state [34,35]. These two peaks of Sm 3d in 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP sample display a slight shift to the low energy region compared with those for SmFeO3/ATP, indicating that the chemical environment of Sm in the CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP has changed. It may be ascribed to the presence of SmOC bonding. Fig. 5(c) shows the Fe 2p XPS spectrum of ATP, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP. It can be found that there appear two peaks around 711 and 724 eV respectively corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks. The result suggests that the Fe species is mainly composed of trivalent Fe3+ in the of composites [36]. Fig. 5(d) shows the typical XPS spectra of Si 2p3/2 centered at 102 eV. These binding energies of SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP exhibit a lower energy shift compared with ATP, suggesting the interaction occurs between ATP and other species [37]. The O 1 s peak at 531 eV for SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP in Fig. 5(e) can be assigned to the oxygen in SmFeO3 crystals. Compared with pure ATP, the O 1 s peak exhibits about 0.8 eV shifts to a lower binding energy, indicating that there is interaction between SmFeO3 and ATP. Fig. 5(f) shows the XPS spectrum of C1s which originates from the CQDs loaded on the SmFeO3/ATP. It can be found that the peaks of 284.82 and 286.47 eV are assigned to the CC/CC of the graphite structure of CQDs, while the binding energy at 288.11 and 289.59 eV can be attributed to the CO and CO bond, respectively [38,39]. Therefore the CQDs are tightly combined with SmFeO3/ATP composite, which is consistent with the aforementioned result.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, SmFeO3/ATP and CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP with different amounts of CQDs are shown in Fig. 6 (a), respectively. It can be seen that the pure ATP has dominant adsorption in the UV range while the pure SmFeO3 has an obvious adsorption edge in visible light range. Compared with SmFeO3/ATP, it can be found that the visible light response capability of CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP composite has been significantly improved, and its absorption edges apparently shift to longer wavelengths correspondingly. In principle, the adsorption edges would be broadened when the amounts of CQDs increased [32]. However, when the CQDs content is larger than 5 wt%, the absorption edge demonstrates an obvious blue shift at present. This phenomenon can be attributed to the excessive CQDs resulting aggregation which reduce the absorption of light. Consequently, the suitable CQDs demonstrate well dispersion on the SmFeO3/ATP surface, which may be beneficial for the photocatalytic performance.

The A(hv)2—hv spectrum profile is calculated from the UV–vis spectra, which can estimate the sample band gap value. The band gap energy is calculated using the equation [40]:
α
h ν
= A
h ν - Eg
n / 2
Where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, A is a proportionality constant and n = 2 which represents a direct transition between VB and CB. The band gap energy spectra of pure ATP and SmFeO3 are shown in Fig. 6(b), corresponding to the band gap energy of 3.75 and 2.52 eV, respectively. According to the UV–vis results, it can be assumed that ATP cannot be excited by visible light because its band gap is too large (3.75 eV) to absorb the visible light [20]. The nanocomposites of CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP have a larger visible light absorption range than SmFeO3 and SmFeO3/ATP. Owning to the introduction of CQDs, the required energy for original electronic transition of the nanocomposites is reduced under visible light, thus improving the absorption of visible light. It can be found that 5 wt% CQDs shows the largest absorption edge, which may be ascribed to the well-defined formation of chemical bonding between ATP and SmFeO3. Therefore, the interfacial charge transfers intermediated by CQDs is responsible for the enhancement of UV and visible light absorption.

The photoluminescence (PL) test is employed to investigate the charge recombination and transfer behavior of the synthesized samples. Generally, lower PL intensity suggests lower recombination of electron-hole pairs [41]. Fig. 7 (a) shows the PL spectra of SmFeO3, ATP, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP under an excitation wavelength of 480 nm. The PL peak at about 602 nm of pure ATP and 648 nm of pure SmFeO3 are identified, which are ascribed to the self-trapped recombination of charge carriers. Compared with SmFeO3 and ATP, the PL intensity of SmFeO3/ATP composite is lower because of the formation of heterojunction between SmFeO3 and ATP, which can efficiently suppress the recombination of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Surprisingly, the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP shows the larger PL intensity than that of SmFeO3/ATP, which can be attributed to higher recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs in the CQDs intermediate [42]. It is anticipated that the CQDs formed on the surface of ATP and SmFeO3 possess suitable band position, and the photogenerated electron in the CB of ATP may transfer to the holes in the VB of SmFeO3 through the CQDs and recombine simultaneously leading to the higher PL intensity [43]. The increased PL signal for 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP could be considered as strong evidence for indirect Z-scheme heterostructure. Therefore, rich electrons left in the CB of SmFeO3 and holes left in the VB of ATP may participate in photocatalytic reaction.

Photocurrent response under light irradiation and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were used to elucidate the photoinduced electrons migration behavior. Fig. 7(b) shows the transient photocurrent response of pure ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP under solar light. In general, high photocurrent intensity implies high separation of photogenerated charge carriers [44]. The results demonstrate that stable and reversible photocurrent is achieved during four on/off cycles of illumination. Compared with ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP revealed a much higher photocurrent, suggesting that the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs were gradually enhanced in the ternary composite. Furthermore, the corresponding curves of EIS are shown in Fig. S3, it can be found that the arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot of 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP is obviously smaller than others, indicating that the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP possesses a lower resistance value for charge transfer. Therefore, the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers of SmFeO3/ATP is dramatically improved by the introduction of the CQDs.

Photocurrent response under light irradiation and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were used to elucidate the photoinduced electrons migration behavior. Fig. 7(b) shows the transient photocurrent response of pure ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP under solar light. In general, high photocurrent intensity implies high separation of photogenerated charge carriers [44]. The results demonstrate that stable and reversible photocurrent is achieved during four on/off cycles of illumination. Compared with ATP, SmFeO3, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP revealed a much higher photocurrent, suggesting that the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs were gradually enhanced in the ternary composite. Furthermore, the corresponding curves of EIS are shown in Fig. S3, it can be found that the arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot of 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP is obviously smaller than others, indicating that the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP possesses a lower resistance value for charge transfer. Therefore, the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers of SmFeO3/ATP is dramatically improved by the introduction of the CQDs.

Fig. 8 shows the photocatalytic oxidation of NO with the various amount of CQDs modified SmFeO3/ATP. The adsorption–desorption balance has been achieved between the catalyst and gas for 10 min adsorption process. Fig. 8(a) shows the NO conversion rate with irradiation time under simulated solar light irradiation, where NO is oxidized to NO3 − by the photogenerated reactive radicals. For comparison, the NO degradation under visible light irradiation for 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP and SmFeO3/ATP are also displayed in Fig. 9 (a). It can be observed that pure ATP has almost 20% of NO degradation owing to its adsorption property for gas molecules and UV responsive band gap. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic activity of pure SmFeO3/ATP is obviously better than ATP. When a small amount of CQDs decorate SmFeO3/ATP, the rate of NO removal could be significantly enhanced. It can be found that NO conversion presents a rising trend and achieve the highest NO removal rate of 90% with the increasing of CQDs amount from 1 wt% to 5 wt%. However, when it increases to 7 wt%, NO conversion rate of the sample decreases. Interestingly, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP of NO removal rate are obviously weaker under visible light irradiation compared with its counterpart under simulated solar light. Therefore, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP samples could be attributed to the heterostructure with intimate contact resulting in the promotion of charge separation. The decreased activity of the samples may be associated with the accumulation of excess CQDs on the catalyst surface [32]. Fig. 8(b) shows the selectivity curves of the CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposites with different CQDs contents compared with SmFeO3/ATP. An obvious increase of curves can be noted with the increasing of CQDs content, whereby the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP affords a maximum NO selectivity of nearly 100%. When the CQDs amount is beyond 5 wt%, NO selectivity of sample decreases. This phenomenon can be rationalized that when the CQDs amount reaches maximum, the excess loading on one hand may inhibit the NO adsorption, on the other hand may deteriorate the charge transfer channel, both of which lead to the decreasing of separation efficiency and the selectivity. In general, the final product of nitrate that accumulate on the surface of the photocatalyst can cause catalyst deactivation which can be easily removed by washing with water [44,45]. Therefore, in order to study the photocatalytic effect of the regenerated catalyst, the corresponding NO conversion efficiency is shown in Fig. S5 which shows nearly 50% conversion after testing three times.

Fig. 8 shows the photocatalytic oxidation of NO with the various amount of CQDs modified SmFeO3/ATP. The adsorption–desorption balance has been achieved between the catalyst and gas for 10 min adsorption process. Fig. 8(a) shows the NO conversion rate with irradiation time under simulated solar light irradiation, where NO is oxidized to NO3 − by the photogenerated reactive radicals. For comparison, the NO degradation under visible light irradiation for 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP and SmFeO3/ATP are also displayed in Fig. 9 (a). It can be observed that pure ATP has almost 20% of NO degradation owing to its adsorption property for gas molecules and UV responsive band gap. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic activity of pure SmFeO3/ATP is obviously better than ATP. When a small amount of CQDs decorate SmFeO3/ATP, the rate of NO removal could be significantly enhanced. It can be found that NO conversion presents a rising trend and achieve the highest NO removal rate of 90% with the increasing of CQDs amount from 1 wt% to 5 wt%. However, when it increases to 7 wt%, NO conversion rate of the sample decreases. Interestingly, SmFeO3/ATP and 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP of NO removal rate are obviously weaker under visible light irradiation compared with its counterpart under simulated solar light. Therefore, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP samples could be attributed to the heterostructure with intimate contact resulting in the promotion of charge separation. The decreased activity of the samples may be associated with the accumulation of excess CQDs on the catalyst surface [32]. Fig. 8(b) shows the selectivity curves of the CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposites with different CQDs contents compared with SmFeO3/ATP. An obvious increase of curves can be noted with the increasing of CQDs content, whereby the 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP affords a maximum NO selectivity of nearly 100%. When the CQDs amount is beyond 5 wt%, NO selectivity of sample decreases. This phenomenon can be rationalized that when the CQDs amount reaches maximum, the excess loading on one hand may inhibit the NO adsorption, on the other hand may deteriorate the charge transfer channel, both of which lead to the decreasing of separation efficiency and the selectivity. In general, the final product of nitrate that accumulate on the surface of the photocatalyst can cause catalyst deactivation which can be easily removed by washing with water [44,45]. Therefore, in order to study the photocatalytic effect of the regenerated catalyst, the corresponding NO conversion efficiency is shown in Fig. S5 which shows nearly 50% conversion after testing three times.

The valence bands of ATP and SmFeO3 were measured by the XPS valence band spectrum as shown in Fig. 9. It can be found that the VB of ATP is determined to be 3.6 eV, while SmFeO3 is determined to be 2.05 eV. Since the band gap of ATP is evaluated to be approximately 3.75 eV, while the SmFeO3 is obtained approximately 2.52 eV from the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 6(b), the CB edge potential (ECB) can be determined by ECB = EVB − Eg [46], while the ECB of ATP and SmFeO3 are estimated to be −0.15 and −0.47 eV. In order to further confirm the effect of active species of ternary composite and to explore the mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction, the electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of the radicals spin-trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) in dispersions are recorded under visible light irradiation and the results are shown in Fig. S4. As presented in Fig. S4(a), the characteristic signals ascribed to DMPO-radO2− could not be seen with visible light irradiation. However, it appeared that four obvious peaks corresponding to DMPO-radOH were detected in Fig. S4b). The ESR tests results confirmed that the radOH rather than radO2− is suggested to play an important role in photocatalytic oxidation NO process under visible light [47,48].

The valence bands of ATP and SmFeO3 were measured by the XPS valence band spectrum as shown in Fig. 9. It can be found that the VB of ATP is determined to be 3.6 eV, while SmFeO3 is determined to be 2.05 eV. Since the band gap of ATP is evaluated to be approximately 3.75 eV, while the SmFeO3 is obtained approximately 2.52 eV from the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 6(b), the CB edge potential (ECB) can be determined by ECB = EVB − Eg [46], while the ECB of ATP and SmFeO3 are estimated to be −0.15 and −0.47 eV. In order to further confirm the effect of active species of ternary composite and to explore the mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction, the electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of the radicals spin-trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) in dispersions are recorded under visible light irradiation and the results are shown in Fig. S4. As presented in Fig. S4(a), the characteristic signals ascribed to DMPO-radO2− could not be seen with visible light irradiation. However, it appeared that four obvious peaks corresponding to DMPO-radOH were detected in Fig. S4(b). The ESR tests results confirmed that the radOH rather than radO2− is suggested to play an important role in photocatalytic oxidation NO process under visible light [47,48].

Based on the above experimental results, two proposed mechanisms for the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposites are shown schematically in Fig. 10 (a, b). As shown in Fig. 10(a), the SmFeO3 could easily be excited to generate electrons and holes by visible light irradiation. The ATP is not activated (λ > 420 nm, energy < 2.95 eV), which is attributed to its wider band gap (3.75 eV) as compared to SmFeO3 (2.52 eV). When SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposite is modified with CQDs, these photogenerated electrons on the CB of SmFeO3 quickly transfer to CQDs and then move to ATP. Although the conjugated structure of CQDs enables efficient separation of electron–hole pairs, the transferred electrons in the CB of ATP (−0.15 eV) is more positive than the potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 eV vs NHE), thus
O2 − could not be produced [23,49]. However, the potential of the holes at the lower-lever VB of SmFeO3 (2.05 eV) is more positive than the redox potential of OH−/
OH (1.99 eV), therefore the holes could directly oxidize OH− to
OH. Only the generated
OH in the VB of SmFeO3 may play role in the removal of NO photocatalytic oxidation under visible light irradiation.

However, under simulated solar light irradiation shown in Fig. 10(b), the electrons of both SmFeO3 and ATP could be excited from the VB to the CB, leaving the holes in the VB, respectively. Normally, since the CB of SmFeO3 is more negative than that of ATP, these photoinduced electrons could transfer from the CB of SmFeO3 to ATP by CQDs. While the VB of ATP is more positive than that of SmFeO3, these photogenerated holes could transfer from the VB of ATP to SmFeO3. However, the transferred electrons in the CB of ATP (−0.15 eV) is more positive than the potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 eV vs NHE), consequently the
O2 − radical cannot be produced. Thus the photocatalytic oxidation efficiency of NO should be very low, which is obviously inconsistent with the experimental results in Fig. 9(a). Therefore, the photoinduced electrons should be transferred from CB of ATP to the VB of SmFeO3 through CQDs as medium, whereas electrons and holes are accumulated in the CB of SmFeO3 and the VB of ATP, respectively. Since the CB of SmFeO3 is more negative than the potentials of O2/
O2 −, the
O2 − can be produced by SmFeO3. In additions, the holes at the VB of ATP (3.5 eV) is more positive than the redox potential of OH−/
OH (1.99 eV), thus the holes can directly oxidize OH− to
OH. Therefore, as proposed in Fig. 10(b), Z-scheme charge transfer process is confirmed in CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP where CQDs behave as the electron mediator. Hence, CQDs play a critical role in effective separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs through indirect Z-scheme heterojunction, which exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity for the removal of NO under simulated sunlight irradiation.

In general, the photocatalytic oxidation removal of NO is attributable to several active species, such as the hydroxyl radicals (
OH), the superoxide (
O2 −) and holes. These active species with strong oxidation capability can preferentially oxidize the NO gas adsorbed on the ATP surface, which could react with those active species to produce the final products of NO3 −. Based on the above investigation, the photocatalytic oxidation reactions removal of NO can be presented in the following reactions: (1)–(7) [7,8,50].
(1) CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP + hv → SmFeO3 (e−) + ATP (h+)
(2) O2 + e− →
O2 −
(3) NO +
O2 − → NO3 −
(4) H2O + h+ →
OH + H+
(5) OH− + h+ →
OH
(6) 2
OH + NO → NO2 + H2O
(7) NO2 +
OH → NO3 − + H+

Novel CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposites have been successfully synthesized via a facile sol-gel/impregnation method. The CQDs with average size 5 nm behaves as intermediate between SmFeO3 and ATP, which promote the interfacial charge transfer and maintain high reduction and oxidation potential as compared with the binary SmFeO3/ATP nanocomposite. Due to the generation of
O2 − and
OH active radicals, 5 wt% CQDs/SmFeO3/ATP achieves as high as 90% for NO conversion and 100% selectivity under simulated solar light irradiation. It is much higher than the counterpart under the visible light irradiation, therefore a Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism for photocatalytic oxidation of NO is proposed. Current work is anticipated to provide a new alternative in developing mineral-based photocatalysts for environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51674043, 51702026), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), Jiangsu Technology Support Program (BE2015103, BE2016654) and Social Development Fund of Changzhou City(CE20165022)
